text: Sustained high zinc intake can deplete copper. Commonly referenced ratio in literature is roughly 8-15:1 (zinc:copper).
Zinc and calcium compete for the same absorption pathway; commonly recommended to space apart.
Zinc and iron compete for absorption; commonly recommended to space apart.
High-dose zinc and magnesium may modestly compete for absorption at the same sitting.
Calcium can inhibit iron absorption when taken together.
High folate intake can mask the blood-test signs of B12 deficiency; both are worth tracking together.
Commonly taken together; D increases calcium absorption, K2 helps direct it to bone.
Both fat-soluble; taking together with a fat-containing meal is efficient for absorption.
Both fat-soluble; pairing with a meal supports absorption of both.
Both fat-soluble; commonly stacked together with a meal.
Vitamin C can enhance non-heme iron absorption when taken together.
Both may have mild blood-thinning effects at high doses; relevant if also on anticoagulant medication.
Both are commonly used for sleep/relaxation; combined sedative effect has limited formal study.
